content:
# Revelation Chapter 11 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 285-286 | **Source Image:** REV_011_page_001.jpg, REV_011_page_002.jpg

1. And there was given to me a reed like a rod, and it was said, "Rise and measure the temple of God and the altar and those who worship in it.

2. And the court which is outside the temple, cast it out and do not measure it, for it has been given to the nations, and they shall trample the holy city for forty-two months.

3. And I will give authority to my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy for one thousand two hundred and sixty days, clothed in sackcloth."

4. These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands which stand before the Lord of the earth.

5. And if anyone wishes to harm them, fire proceeds out of their mouth and devours their enemies; and if anyone should wish to harm them, thus must he be killed.

6. These have the authority to shut heaven, so that rain may not fall during the days of their prophecy; and they have authority over the waters to turn them into blood, and to strike the earth with every plague, as often as they desire.

7. And when they have finished their testimony, the beast that comes up out of the abyss shall make war with them, and shall overcome them, and shall kill them.

8. And their dead body shall lie in the street of the great city, which is called spiritually Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.

9. And those from the peoples and tribes and tongues and nations look upon their dead bodies for three and a half days, and they do not permit their dead bodies to be placed in a tomb.

10. And those who dwell upon the earth rejoice over them and make merry, and they shall send gifts to one another, because these two prophets tormented those who dwell upon the earth.

11. And after the three and a half days, a spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet, and great fear fell upon those who beheld them.

12. And they heard a loud voice from heaven saying to them, "Come up here." And they went up into heaven in the cloud, and their enemies beheld them.

13. And in that hour there was a great earthquake, and a tenth of the city fell, and seven thousand people were killed in the earthquake, and the rest became afraid and gave glory to the God of heaven.

14. The second woe has passed; behold, the third woe comes quickly.

15. And the seventh angel sounded his trumpet, and there were loud voices in heaven, saying, "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever."

16. And the twenty-four elders who sit upon their thrones before God fell upon their faces and worshipped God,

17. saying, "We give thanks to you, Lord God Almighty, who is and who was, because you have taken your great power and have begun to reign.

18. And the nations were wrathful, and your wrath came, and the time for the dead to be judged, and to give the reward to your servants the prophets and to the saints and to those who fear your name, the small and the great, and to destroy those who destroy the earth."

19. And the temple of God which is in heaven was opened, and the ark of his covenant was seen in his temple; and there were flashes of lightning and voices and thunders and an earthquake and great hail.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Verse 4:** All three translators agree: the two witnesses are identified as the two olive trees and the two lampstands standing before the Lord of the earth.

**Verse 5:** All three render the conditional threat with essentially identical structure — fire from their mouth, enemies devoured, anyone who harms them must die likewise.

**Verse 14:** All three agree on the substance: the second woe has passed, the third comes quickly.

**Verse 16:** All three agree: the twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship God.

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1 — "saying" vs. "it was said" vs. "was told":**
- EMET: "a reed like a rod, saying, 'Rise and measure the temple of God'"
- LOGOS: "a reed like a rod, and it was said, 'Rise and measure the temple of God'"
- PNEUMA: "a measuring rod like a staff and was told, 'Go and measure the temple of God and the altar, and count those who worship there'"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "it was said" resolves the awkward grammatical issue (legōn modifying kalamos) without attributing speech to the reed itself (EMET). PNEUMA's "measuring rod" and "staff" reverse the Greek comparison (kalamos homoios rhabdō — a reed like a rod, not a rod like a staff). PNEUMA's "count" for "measure" (metrēson) is interpretive; the same verb governs all three objects.

**Verse 2 — "cast out" vs. "exclude"; "was given" vs. "has been given" vs. "handed over":**
- EMET: "the court which [is] outside the temple cast out and do not measure it, for it was given to the nations"
- LOGOS: "the court which is outside the temple, cast it out and do not measure it, for it has been given to the nations"
- PNEUMA: "exclude the outer courtyard — do not measure it — because it has been handed over to the nations"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Cast it out" preserves the imperative ekbale. "Has been given" (LOGOS) better reflects the Greek perfect/aorist passive than "was given" (EMET). PNEUMA's "exclude" and "handed over" are interpretive — ekbale means "cast out," and edothē is "was given," not "handed over."

**Verse 3 — "I shall give [authority]" vs. "I will give authority" vs. "I will empower"; number format:**
- EMET: "I shall give [authority] to my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred sixty days"
- LOGOS: "I will give authority to my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy for one thousand two hundred and sixty days"
- PNEUMA: "I will empower my two witnesses to prophesy for 1,260 days"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "I will give authority" is faithful to dōsō with implied exousian. PNEUMA's "empower" is interpretive, and the numeral "1,260" breaks the formal register. EMET's "shall give" is slightly archaic.

**Verse 6 — "shut the heaven" vs. "shut heaven" vs. "shut the sky"; "as often as they may wish" vs. "desire" vs. "wish":**
- EMET: "to shut the heaven, that rain may not fall [during] the days of their prophecy...as often as they may wish"
- LOGOS: "to shut heaven, so that rain may not fall during the days of their prophecy...as often as they desire"
- PNEUMA: "to shut the sky so that no rain falls during the time they are prophesying...as often as they wish"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Heaven" (without article) matches standard English idiom for ouranos in this usage. "Sky" (PNEUMA) is too mundane for the apocalyptic register. "Desire" (LOGOS) is adopted for thelōsin.

**Verse 7 — "finish" vs. "have finished" vs. "have finished delivering"; "beast...out of the abyss" vs. "bottomless pit":**
- EMET: "when they finish their testimony, the beast that comes up out of the abyss"
- LOGOS: "when they have finished their testimony, the beast that comes up out of the abyss"
- PNEUMA: "When they have finished delivering their testimony, the beast that rises from the bottomless pit"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Have finished" renders the aorist subjunctive telesōsin properly. PNEUMA's "delivering" is added, and "bottomless pit" interprets abyssos (see ch. 9 discussion).

**Verse 8 — "corpse" vs. "dead bodies" vs. "bodies"; "spiritually" vs. "figuratively":**
- EMET: "their corpse [shall be] upon the street of the great city, which is called spiritually Sodom and Egypt"
- LOGOS: "their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city, which is called spiritually Sodom and Egypt"
- PNEUMA: "Their bodies will lie in the main street of the great city — which is figuratively called Sodom and Egypt"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA renders "their dead body" (singular, following EMET's note that Sinaiticus reads singular ptōma with plural autōn), with "shall lie" for the implied verb. "Spiritually" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained over "figuratively" (PNEUMA) — pneumatikōs derives from pneuma and carries theological weight beyond mere literary figure.

**Verse 9 — "see" vs. "look upon" vs. "stare at"; "three days and a half" vs. "three and a half days":**
- EMET: "see their corpse three days and a half, and they do not permit their corpses to be placed into a tomb"
- LOGOS: "look upon their dead bodies for three and a half days, and they do not permit their dead bodies to be placed in a tomb"
- PNEUMA: "stare at their bodies and refuse to let them be buried"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Look upon" for blepousin is precise. "Three and a half days" is natural English word order. PNEUMA's "stare" and "refuse to let them be buried" are interpretive.

**Verse 10 — "those dwelling upon the earth" vs. "those who dwell" vs. "those living on the earth":**
- EMET: "those dwelling upon the earth rejoice over them and make merry"
- LOGOS: "those who dwell upon the earth rejoice over them and make merry"
- PNEUMA: "Those living on the earth will celebrate over them and exchange gifts"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Those who dwell" is the standard rendering of hoi katoikountes. PNEUMA's "celebrate" and "exchange gifts" are dynamic paraphrases of chairousin, euphranontai, and pempsousin dōra.

**Verse 11 — "[the] spirit of life" vs. "a spirit of life" vs. "the breath of life":**
- EMET: "[the] spirit of life from God entered into them"
- LOGOS: "a spirit of life from God entered into them"
- PNEUMA: "the breath of life from God entered them"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "a spirit of life" renders pneuma zōēs without the definite article (which Sinaiticus omits). PNEUMA's "breath" is interpretive, though valid for pneuma; "spirit" preserves the ambiguity.

**Verse 12 — "great voice" vs. "loud voice"; "out of the heaven" vs. "from heaven":**
- EMET: "a great voice out of the heaven"
- LOGOS: "a loud voice from heaven"
- PNEUMA: "a loud voice from heaven"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA — "loud voice from heaven" is natural and faithful.

**Verse 13 — "names of men seven thousand" vs. "seven thousand names of men" vs. "Seven thousand people":**
- EMET: "there were killed in the earthquake names of men seven thousand"
- LOGOS: "seven thousand names of men were killed in the earthquake"
- PNEUMA: "Seven thousand people were killed in the earthquake"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA renders "seven thousand people were killed" — adopting the clearest English word order. "Names of men" (onomata anthrōpōn) is a Hebraism meaning "persons/people," which PNEUMA correctly interprets. EMET's word order follows the Greek but is unnatural in English. SOPHIA notes the Hebraism in deference to EMET and LOGOS but prioritizes comprehension.

**Verse 15 — "Christos" vs. "Christ"; "unto the ages of the ages" vs. "forever and ever":**
- EMET: "of his Christos, and he shall reign unto the ages of the ages"
- LOGOS: "of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever"
- PNEUMA: "of his Christ, and he will reign forever and ever"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Christ" is the standard English form of Christos. "Forever and ever" renders eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn in established English idiom. EMET's transliteration "Christos" and literal "unto the ages of the ages" are hyper-literal.

**Verse 17 — "Lord God the Almighty" vs. "Lord God Almighty":**
- EMET: "Lord God the Almighty, the [One] who is and the [One] who was"
- LOGOS: "Lord God Almighty, who is and who was"
- PNEUMA: "Lord God, the Almighty, the One who is and who was"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Lord God Almighty" without extra articles is the standard English rendering of Kyrie ho theos ho pantokratōr.

**Verse 18 — "holy ones" vs. "saints" vs. "holy people":**
- EMET: "to the holy ones and to those fearing your name"
- LOGOS: "to the saints and to those who fear your name"
- PNEUMA: "your holy people and all who honor your name"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "saints" is the established rendering of hagiois in formal translation. PNEUMA's "honor" for phoboumenois (fearing) softens the Greek.

**Verse 19 — "lightnings" vs. "flashes of lightning"; "voices and thunders" ordering:**
- EMET: "there were lightnings and voices and thunders and an earthquake and great hail"
- LOGOS: "there were flashes of lightning and voices and thunders and an earthquake and great hail"
- PNEUMA: "there came flashes of lightning, rumblings, peals of thunder, an earthquake, and a severe hailstorm"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Flashes of lightning" (LOGOS) is more natural than bare "lightnings" (EMET). PNEUMA adds "rumblings" and "peals of" which go beyond the Greek phōnai (voices) and brontai (thunders), and "severe hailstorm" interprets chalaza megalē beyond "great hail."

---

*Signed, SOPHIA — in wisdom, seeking the faithful center where precision meets clarity*
